additional_information.if_so_who: Maine Bureau of Veterans' Services and Penobscot Community Health Care social work department

explanation_of_need.describe_the_financial_hardship_that_you:
I am requesting emergency financial assistance because I am currently two months behind on rent and at immediate risk of eviction, while also struggling to keep up with utility bills and basic necessities for my family.

I live in a rented two-bedroom apartment at 214 Oak Hill Road, Apt 3B, in Bangor with my two children: my daughter Emily (age 9) and my son Jacob (age 6). I share 50/50 custody with their mother, but the children are with me slightly more than half of the time due to her shift work. I am the primary person responsible for paying our household expenses when the kids are with me.

Until February of this year, I was working full-time as a warehouse supervisor for a regional distribution company in Brewer, earning about $22 per hour plus occasional overtime. In late February, the company downsized and eliminated my position along with several others. I received a small severance and used my tax refund to keep up with rent and utilities through April, but those funds have now been exhausted.

I was able to find new employment in late May as a delivery driver for a local building supply company. However, this position is only 30–32 hours per week on average, at $17.50 per hour, and does not offer benefits. My hours are dependent on deliveries and have been inconsistent, especially on rainy weeks when construction slows down. I am actively trying to get more hours, but the company has told me this may not be possible until next spring. As a result, my monthly income dropped significantly compared to my previous job.

In addition to the loss of income, I had several unexpected expenses over the last few months:

1. Car repairs: In June, the transmission in my 2010 Honda CR-V started slipping and the car became unsafe to drive. Because I rely on this vehicle for my job as a delivery driver and to transport my kids, I had no choice but to get it repaired. The repair cost $1,486.27 (I have the invoice), which I had to put on a high-interest credit card. The minimum monthly payment on that card is now around $85, which I am struggling to meet.

2. Medical and dental costs: I receive care through the VA, but my children are on MaineCare. Emily needed an emergency dental visit in July due to a cracked molar and then a follow-up filling that was only partially covered, leaving me with a $190 bill. At around the same time, Jacob had an asthma flare-up that required an urgent care visit and a new inhaler prescription. While most of it was covered, I still had about $60 in co-pays and medication costs. These were not expenses I had budgeted for.

3. Utility arrears: My CMP electric bill has gotten behind. I had to choose between paying enough to keep the lights on and paying full rent. Over the last three months, I have underpaid rent to cover food and gas so I could keep working and make sure the kids had what they needed for school. As of this application, I owe approximately $1,450 in back rent (July and August full rent plus a late fee, with September partially paid) and about $275 on my electric bill that is now in disconnect status if I don’t make a payment arrangement soon.

My monthly expenses are pretty bare-bones. My rent is $975 per month. Electricity averages $120–$150 but has been higher this summer with fans running. I spend around $450–$500 per month on groceries when the kids are with me, even using coupons and food pantries occasionally. Gas for the car is about $200–$250 monthly given my delivery job and driving the kids to school and appointments. I also pay $65 for a basic phone plan and $55 for internet, which my daughter especially needs for schoolwork.

Because of the gap between my previous income and my current reduced hours, I am now in a situation where I simply cannot catch up without help. I have already used up my small savings, sold a few items (including an old TV and some tools) to make partial payments, and borrowed a small amount from a friend that I am paying back slowly. My landlord has posted a formal notice and has told me that if I don’t bring the rent current or at least make a significant payment toward the balance within the next two weeks, he will proceed with eviction. I have lived here for almost four years and have never been late before the job loss.

I am doing my best to manage, but the combination of reduced work hours, unexpected car and medical expenses, and the rising cost of everything has put me in a serious bind. I am especially worried about the impact this instability is having on my kids—my daughter has been asking if we are going to have to move, and my son has been more anxious at night. I am applying to this program because I need a one-time boost to get caught up on rent and utilities so we can stay in our home while I continue working and looking for a second job or a better full-time opportunity.

In short, the emergency is that I am behind on rent and utilities due to a layoff and underemployment, compounded by necessary car repairs and medical expenses. Without assistance, there is a very real risk that my children and I will lose our housing within the next month.

long_term_plan.what_steps_are_you_taking_to_prevent_a_s:
Over the past few months, I have been taking a number of steps to stabilize my situation and make sure I don’t end up in this kind of emergency again. My goal is to get back to a steady, full-time income with some cushion for emergencies so that a single unexpected bill doesn’t put my family at risk of eviction.

1. Employment and income plan:
- I am actively looking for a full-time job that better matches my skills and prior experience. Before my layoff, I worked as a warehouse supervisor for eight years, and I also have logistics and inventory management experience from my time in the Army. I have updated my resume with the help of a counselor at the Maine CareerCenter in Bangor, and I’ve already applied to several positions, including warehouse lead roles at a major home improvement store and a regional logistics company in Hermon.
- In the meantime, I am doing everything I can to increase my hours with my current employer. I have told my supervisor that I am available for any extra routes, weekend deliveries, or fill-in shifts when other drivers call out. Over the last month, this has given me an extra 4–6 hours per week on average, and I’m hoping that will continue to grow.
- I have also started picking up occasional gig work on weekends, such as grocery delivery and food delivery through app-based services. While the income from this varies, it has helped me bring in an extra $150–$200 in some weeks, which I am using strictly for past-due bills and building a small emergency cushion.

2. Budgeting and debt management:
- I worked with a financial counselor at Penobscot Community Health Care’s social work department to create a detailed monthly budget. We went line by line through my expenses and identified areas where I could cut back. For example, I cancelled a small streaming service subscription and a gym membership I was barely using, and I switched to a cheaper phone plan. These changes saved me about $60–$70 per month.
- I contacted my credit card company (the one I used for the car repairs) and enrolled in a hardship program that temporarily lowered my interest rate and fixed my monthly payment at a more manageable amount. I have committed to paying at least the minimum every month, and as my income stabilizes, I plan to pay extra toward the principal.
- I have set a specific goal of building a small emergency fund of at least $500 over the next 6–8 months. My plan is to set aside $20–$30 from each paycheck, even if it’s a stretch, into a separate savings account. This will give me something to fall back on if another unexpected expense comes up, like a car repair or a medical co-pay.

3. Housing stability:
- I have spoken with my landlord directly and been honest about my situation. I provided him with documentation of my layoff and my current job, and we discussed a realistic payment plan to stay current once I am caught up. He has been willing to work with me as long as he sees that I am making consistent payments and communicating regularly.
- I am working with the Maine Bureau of Veterans’ Services to explore any rental assistance programs, utility relief options, or other resources that can help keep my housing stable in the short term. They have already helped me understand what documentation is needed and connected me with a local nonprofit that may be able to assist with budgeting and tenant–landlord mediation if needed.

4. Education and skills improvement:
- I have begun looking into short-term training programs that could help me move into a higher-paying and more stable field, such as CDL (commercial driver’s license) training or a certificate in supply chain management. I have talked with a VA representative about whether any of this could be supported through my remaining education benefits. My hope is that by increasing my qualifications, I can secure a job with better pay and benefits, which would greatly reduce the risk of future financial crises.

5. Support systems and mental health:
- I have started meeting once a month with a counselor at the VA clinic in Bangor to help manage stress, anxiety, and some lingering issues related to my deployments. When I get overwhelmed, I tend to shut down and avoid dealing with bills, which only makes things worse. Working with a counselor is helping me stay on top of communication with creditors and my landlord and avoid letting problems snowball.
- I am also making more use of local community resources, like the food pantry at our local church, especially during weeks when my hours are low. This helps stretch my grocery budget so that more of my cash can go toward rent and utilities.

6. Practical daily changes:
- I have cut back on driving that isn’t work- or kid-related to save on gas. I combine errands into a single trip whenever possible.
- At home, I’ve been more careful about electricity use—turning off lights, using fans instead of air conditioning, and running the washer and dryer only when I have full loads. It sounds small, but it has lowered my CMP bill by a bit over the last two billing cycles.

In summary, my long-term plan is to: stabilize my housing by catching up on rent and staying in regular contact with my landlord; increase and diversify my income through more hours, side work, and a better-paying job; stick to a realistic budget and gradually pay down debt; build an emergency fund; and continue using support services (VA, Maine Bureau of Veterans’ Services, and local nonprofits) to maintain both financial and emotional stability. I am not looking for ongoing assistance—what I need is a one-time push to get out of this immediate crisis so that the steps I am already taking can actually work.
